words: This conversation with "MICHIGAN" BILL STOWELL, took place at his home in Lewiston,
Idaho on February 24, 1976.
SAM SCHRAGER: I was wondering about this in the old days with the lumberjacks.
They'd stay in the woods for a pretty long time before they'd get to
go in town?
WILLIAM STOWELL: Oh, yeah. Three, four, five, six, seven, eight months, you
know.

speaker: SS
words: That seems like a hell of a long time to be out in the boondocks.

speaker: WS
words: Oh, God, yes. I used to do it. But there was always— there was no

speaker: WS
words: TVs, of course, there was poker games. And sometimes if you played

speaker: WS
words: poker you might have to stay. You know, they'd keep you there, if

speaker: WS
words: you weren't winning. See what I mean?

speaker: SS
words: You could lose your stake pretty easy in a game.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, there was a good game there all the time. I used to play all

speaker: WS
words: time, but sometimes— well, you go six to eight months that you

speaker: WS
words: wouldn't win, you'd lose. You wouldn't warm the chair even, when you'd

speaker: WS
words: on it. But then again, you'd get lucky and win a thousand and

speaker: WS
words: away you went!

speaker: SS
words: Were the games pretty honest?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, always, yeah. Once in a while a slicker would move in, but, oh,

speaker: WS
words: 1 he'd win of course, but they were easy games to win in, you know,

speaker: WS
words: never^iose, free and easy, you know. Four or five hundredApot, you

speaker: WS
words: might win on a high card, you know: Queen, King, Ace, something like

speaker: WS
words: that. No pairs or nothings they just tried to bluff their way out.

speaker: SS
words: Did you usually play the same kind of poker?

speaker: WS
words: Mostly stud, yeah. Five cards. That was the going deal.

speaker: SS
words: That's a good game.

speaker: WS
words: Oh, Yeah. Fast game.

speaker: SS
words: Well, how did they pay you then, in the woods? Would you get paid by

speaker: SS
words: the week or by the month?

speaker: WS
words: No. Some camps paid when you quit. If you stayed one month, two months,

speaker: WS
words: three months or six months, or no matter how long, they'd pay when you

speaker: WS
words: left. Otherwise it was every month, like the Potlatch did. They paid

speaker: WS
words: every months. And they insisted on you picking up your check, you see.

speaker: WS
words: You know; payday. You could stay, of course, as long as you want to

speaker: WS
words: but they didn't like them checks laying around, you know. Pick it up

speaker: WS
words: every month. That's the way they worked it. But some of them gypos

speaker: WS
words: you could work as long as you wanted to without that, you see. Get it

speaker: WS
words: all when you left. One check.

speaker: SS
words: But if you were going to be gambling, you need some money.

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yes, all the time. You could draw, you know.

speaker: SS
words: Draw off your pay.

speaker: WS
words: Well, the Potlatch had a draw day; the fifteenth. That was a pretty

speaker: WS
words: strict deal. You couldn't draw otherwise. You could draw once a month,

speaker: WS
words: besides your regular payday. That's the way that worked.

speaker: SS
words: Did everybody get in on the gambling or was there a bunch of guys that

speaker: SS
words: wouldn't touch it?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, no. Some didn't gamble at all. Just— well, there was a regular

speaker: WS
words: bunch that— 'course there'd be some that'd drop in an lose what they

speaker: WS
words: had and then they wouldn't play again, you know, for another month

speaker: WS
words: unless they borrowed or got some. But there was a bunch that played

speaker: WS
words: all the time. They were the old, you know—well, they were lucky to

speaker: WS
words: have money and they just played every night. Sometimes like Saturday

speaker: WS
words: night they'd play all Saturday night and all day Sunday, and then fly

speaker: WS
words: at her again, Monday morning. But them games, they don't— there's

speaker: WS
words: no more games now. Not when I left there even. There was a little

speaker: WS
words: game at Headquarters, but it didn't amount to nothing. They— oh, a

speaker: WS
words: few had money, you know. But a small game, didn't amount to nothing.

speaker: WS
words: I never played there.

speaker: SS
words: Would you figure, that when you gambled, would you figure you'd come

speaker: SS
words: out even, or better, or did you come out flat more than—

speaker: WS
words: Well, I think I broke about even. But sometimes I'd go for six to

speaker: WS
words: eight months I wouldn't — as I said— I wouldn't warm the chair

speaker: WS
words: you know. I'd drop everything in a pot or two, you know. We wasn't

speaker: WS
words: getting much, well, a hundred and fifty dollars was a big months, you

speaker: WS
words: know. But that was years ago, you know. That was a real, real good

speaker: WS
words: month. A couple of hands'd take that sometimes if you happened to

speaker: WS
words: get mixed up right with 'em. But I didn't borrow, I'd quit. Wait

speaker: WS
words: for another month. (Chuckles) Go at her again! But I usually come

speaker: WS
words: out about even.

speaker: SS
words: What about the honesty in the camps? I've heard that lumberjacks were

speaker: SS
words: honest as the day is long.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, they was. You could— well, you could leave a thousand dollars

speaker: WS
words: lay on your bunk and go to work. Someone might come along and put it

speaker: WS
words: under your pillow or something, but they wouldn't take it. I don't

speaker: WS
words: know how it is now. I imagine it has changed some, but... But I don't

speaker: WS
words: know how— the lumberjack as a rule is a pretty fair guy.

speaker: SS
words: What about debts? Paying back money that you owed?

speaker: WS
words: Well, they--

speaker: SS
words: Men pretty good with that?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yes. Yeah. They were good pay, all of 'em. Might take a little

speaker: WS
words: time, you know. But they'd get it. Might be five or six hundred

speaker: WS
words: miles from there, further, but they'd either send it or see that you

speaker: WS
words: got it, if they owed you. Lumberjacks traveled around a lot them

speaker: WS
words: days, you know. On the coast and back here, Montana and all over.

speaker: WS
words: Gallivanting around.

speaker: SS
words: Did the men have places that they liked specially that they'd stay

speaker: SS
words: at mostly?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yes. They'd run around for a while and then they'd come back to

speaker: WS
words: this spot, you see, where they liked to work. Where they liked the

speaker: WS
words: crew and one thing and another, and settle down. They'd

speaker: WS
words: quite a hitch, as a rule, that's the way it works. Way it always did

speaker: WS
words: with me. I'd run around a while and then I'd finally go back to

speaker: WS
words: where I liked the best. This Potlatch usad to be a good outfit to

speaker: WS
words: work for— still is, I guess. I worked for 'em well, when they was

speaker: WS
words: building Headquarters. I'd been out in this country quite a while

speaker: WS
words: before that, but I was on the Coast quite a bit then. Montana. But

speaker: WS
words: when they started up, I used to always work here sometime during the

speaker: WS
words: year I'd be here for a while. (What are you doing? Writing a book

speaker: WS
words: or something?)

speaker: SS
words: We're hoping to, eventually.

speaker: WS
words: Someone else beside you?

speaker: SS
words: Well, I kind of work for the county; Latah County. But that's what

speaker: SS
words: I'm hoping to be able to do that eventually. 'Cause we've talked

speaker: SS
words: to a lot of people, about the interesting things from everybody.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, see what you mean.

speaker: SS
words: Because you know, you look at it from all the different angles, all

speaker: SS
words: the different kind of people, you know. It's a hell of a story.

speaker: WS
words: condense it down,

speaker: SS
words: I've been working for instead of the Coast, and what made this

speaker: SS
words: look—

speaker: WS
words: Well, all those different reasons. awful heavy on the Coast,

speaker: WS
words: And I never followed no machinery They had machines on the Coast

speaker: WS
words: when I went there. But I never drove no machines. I always worked

speaker: WS
words: on the ground rigging and like of that, you know. I don't know, they

speaker: WS
words: eliminated camps over there first. They were the first to eliminate

speaker: WS
words: the camps, see, like here, they kept 'em, you know. And you could go

speaker: WS
words: to camp and stay there in camp. Over there, they got to drive a long

speaker: WS
words: ways, and married. So, that was one reason why I left there. But

speaker: WS
words: the Rainier, has still got camps over there, but that's about all. And

speaker: WS
words: there's camps in Alaska, too. But, this country here kept the camps

speaker: WS
words: the longest of any.

speaker: SS
words: What about the men? Did you find that the men around here— I mean

speaker: SS
words: did you get to know 'em pretty good? Feel, you know, like they were

speaker: SS
words: friends?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yes. Sure. But, well, I had on the Coast, too. They were

speaker: WS
words: tramps, like myself, you know. They were here and there and every

speaker: WS
words: where. But, of course, there's good people wherever you go. And

speaker: WS
words: then there is some that's not so good, but I never had no connections

speaker: WS
words: with them. You know, if I didn't like 'em I didn't mix with 'em

speaker: WS
words: that's all. But they were all good guys. Very good. I don't know.

speaker: WS
words: I always catered to the short log, I mean I was— river driving, you

speaker: WS
words: see. There wasn't much of that on the coast. Very, very little driving.

speaker: WS
words: It was all rafting, you know. And ere was long logs there

speaker: WS
words: when there was shorter logs here. Always donkey work and I didn't go

speaker: WS
words: for that too much, you know. I liked this short log country; horses,

speaker: WS
words: camps and driving, you know. I used to work on the Coast and I'd come

speaker: WS
words: over here every spring to drive someplace, you know. And you'd meet

speaker: WS
words: guys you hadn't seen for a year. There was several drives in this

speaker: WS
words: country.

speaker: SS
words: Besides the Clearwater?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, Chtist, yes,, lots of 'em.

speaker: SS
words: Where were they driving?

speaker: WS
words: Well, there was the Coeur d'Alene River, Priest River, Wenatchee Riv

speaker: WS
words: er and the Blackfoot in Montana. Priest River. Oh, I can't remember.

speaker: SS
words: Gee, I didn't know there was that many.

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Clearwater is the one you always hear about.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah. Well, that was the last one, you know. You see, years ago

speaker: WS
words: there was no roads in them camps, you know. You hiked in. They were

speaker: WS
words: flume camps. You know, there was no trucks or cars or nothing, and

speaker: WS
words: they flumed everything. You've seen a flume, of course.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: WS
words: Well, they flumed everything into the river and drove it in the

speaker: WS
words: spring. That's the way they did that. But lately, of course, rail

speaker: WS
words: roads and trucks; it's all trucks now course.

speaker: SS
words: Were you in on that Beaver Creek flume?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yeah, I helped build it. 1928.

speaker: SS
words: How did they organize that whole job there? It looks like a big un

speaker: SS
words: dertaking, when you see what's left of it.

speaker: WS
words: Yes. Five miles to start with and they extended.it, of course, later

speaker: WS
words: on. But the original flume was about done for then.

speaker: SS
words: There was an older flume in there?

speaker: WS
words: Well, from Beaver Creek Dam to the river. That was five miles. That

speaker: WS
words: was built first, then there was flumes and chutes into that, you see.

speaker: WS
words: Into the pond, from different camps around there, you know, P, and N

speaker: WS
words: and W, J, and all them camps they all went into Beaver Creek, you know

speaker: WS
words: and flumed from there to the river. But that's been quite a while

speaker: WS
words: ago. I worked there on that Beaver Creek plume in 1941. That's the

speaker: WS
words: last time they used it. They had about, oh, I don't know— we were

speaker: WS
words: in there right after Christmas, and they trucked to that flume. They

speaker: WS
words: had a bunch of Chev y trucks and they trucked to the flume. And that

speaker: WS
words: was just before the war. And we flumed 'em right in, too. 'Course,

speaker: WS
words: there was always lots of water in Beaver Creek, the flume, you know.

speaker: WS
words: You never had trouble with that, even in the summer? They flumed there

speaker: WS
words: all the time?

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, any time. That's a big creek, you know. You been there?

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, I've seen it. It is a big creek. The feeders are pretty good

speaker: WS
words: too.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah. Well, they'd back that water up and hold it, you know. They

speaker: WS
words: had, one, two— two big dams, Beaver Creek Dam and Dam. That

speaker: WS
words: was a way up, 0 and P and them camps come into that. That was above

speaker: WS
words: camp M. That was probably ten miles from the river, or eleven. But

speaker: WS
words: they had a big dam there and they held water there, too.

speaker: SS
words: How much water—? I've never seen a flume running. How much water

speaker: SS
words: would you keep in the flume?

speaker: WS
words: All you could get.

speaker: SS
words: Just fill it right up to the top.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah. 'Course they knew when they built 'em, how much they'd need,

speaker: WS
words: and all this and that. But sometimes in the summer— Beaver Creek

speaker: WS
words: never was that way. There was always water, you know, you could flume.

speaker: WS
words: But, of course, it would take more water to sluice a log through a

speaker: WS
words: dam than it does to roll it in, you know. It takes ten times as

speaker: WS
words: much. But, I worked on them flumes, like Camp T, in the summertime

speaker: WS
words: in mid August or so, you'd get about four floods a day, and no sluicing

speaker: WS
words: except through one dam. They'd Kabout three minute floods, you see.

speaker: WS
words: You had to get that timber in— and you'd maybe flume three hundred

speaker: WS
words: thousand a day, you see, that's board feet, you know. But you'd have

speaker: WS
words: to get that in in twelve minutes. Four three minute floods, two

speaker: WS
words: in the morning and two in the afternoon.

speaker: SS
words: Well, you could have all the timber right by the flood—

speaker: WS
words: Right there, yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Ready to go.

speaker: WS
words: You'd have what you was going to flume. They're all ready. And

speaker: WS
words: then call for the water, you see. They opened the dam and give you

speaker: WS
words: the water.

speaker: SS
words: Gee, they must have moved fast.

speaker: WS
words: Oh, boyl There'd about six, seven men rolling in, you know. On the

speaker: WS
words: average landing where there was maybe seventy-five or aliundred thou

speaker: WS
words: sand each fluming, you see. That's about what you'd have. But there'd

speaker: WS
words: be four or five of them landings, you know. Maybe trucking to some

speaker: WS
words: of 'em, and some of 'em they'd be skidding to.

speaker: SS
words: During that three minutes when it was open you'd have to roll the

speaker: SS
words: lumber right onto the flume right then?

speaker: WS
words: Right then.

speaker: SS
words: How many guys would be working on one landing?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, five or six.

speaker: SS
words: Would they have peavies?

speaker: WS
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Must have been a scramble.

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yeah. You had to watch your step, too, you know. Short water,

speaker: WS
words: you see, and you couldn't double 'em up or nothing, you know, or they'd

speaker: WS
words: jam and there you are and you'd lose the flood and everything. You

speaker: WS
words: had to keep 'em singled out. Oh, yeah, it was quite an undertaking,

speaker: WS
words: but of course, everybody knew this business,^there was nothing to it.

speaker: SS
words: How big were the logs?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, they'd average maybe three, four hundred feet. Course, they'd

speaker: WS
words: be some with a thousand feet in 'em, you know. Some with a hundred

speaker: WS
words: feet in 'em. The average log maybe three hundred feet. They'd just

speaker: WS
words: leave the minute they hit that flume, you know. You concentrate

speaker: WS
words: that water there in one small spot you know, and it's got a lot of

speaker: WS
words: power. You know how them flumes was built.

speaker: SS
words: Yes.

speaker: WS
words: See'a log there was five hundred feet in the chute, get another

speaker: WS
words: one right behind it, and that would be gone a long ways. Yeah. I

speaker: WS
words: Used to like that kind of work.

speaker: SS
words: Would you ever run into trouble with timing with other landings?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yes, well, they had to watch the ones below, you know. They

speaker: WS
words: had to watch real close. The guy on the upper end^ he didn't have to

speaker: WS
words: be too particular to let 'em go, but the guys down below f they

speaker: WS
words: had to watch or they'd be in trouble. But there never was much trou

speaker: WS
words: ble. They all knew their business.

speaker: SS
words: How much of the season did they flume? Did they keep that going right

speaker: SS
words: through the spring and summer?

speaker: WS
words: Yeah. Well, in the wintertime, when they worked in the wintertime,

speaker: WS
words: they would— there was no water in the wintertime, it would freeze up.

speaker: WS
words: They'd deck it. Pile 'em up, you know. And they'd go in the spring

speaker: WS
words: before the drive started and flume 'em. Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: On these river drives— now, I was thinking about that working in

speaker: SS
words: the water— cold in the spring— I imagine that'd be really rugged

speaker: SS
words: work.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, it's tough. Sure.

speaker: SS
words: You got all them wool clothes on and everything, too.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah. The deeper it got the colder it got. Well, some guys could

speaker: WS
words: wade deep, you know and some couldn't. Just a matter of— there

speaker: WS
words: wasn't a hell, of a lot to know about it. Just get in there and go.

speaker: WS
words: 'Course, there was something, too—you had to savvy a little bit

speaker: WS
words: about it in order to do it, but that was just plain old work. That's

speaker: WS
words: all that was. I didn't care much for that part of it. I'd rather

speaker: WS
words: work on the timber, you know, like a center or^that work.

speaker: SS
words: What?

speaker: WS
words: Like a center, you know, something builds up in the middle of the riv

speaker: WS
words: er, and you got to pick 'em off, as a rule. Like this river here,

speaker: WS
words: down along the highway here, the main river, you know, you had to

speaker: WS
words: take all that stuff off by hand, them timbers, you know, on account

speaker: WS
words: of— well, they used machines late years, but you couldn't use pow

speaker: WS
words: der on account of the highway on one side, you know, and the railroad

speaker: WS
words: on the other.Well, it was machine work or handwork, depends on the kind

speaker: WS
words: of water it was. Good big water, why, they'd take 'em off by hand.

speaker: WS
words: Just get their side picking, you know, and loosen em up, get

speaker: WS
words: slack in it and pretty soon she'd all go, you know. You had to

speaker: WS
words: for the boat then. Yeah, that's the way that worked. Used to be a

speaker: WS
words: lot of centers in this river here. All kinds of 'em.

speaker: SS
words: When you say you pick at it; you just tried to separate— just tried

speaker: SS
words: like to— get them logs untangled?

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, that's it. That's just it. Get 'em unraveled. The center al

speaker: WS
words: ways builds up against something, you know. It depends upon what kind

speaker: WS
words: of water that goes on. Different stages of water will cause centers

speaker: WS
words: and some it won't, you know. But, it'll build up on something. It'll

speaker: WS
words: start like on a rock, or an island or a bar or something and it'll build

speaker: WS
words: way back, you know. Well, maybe back fifty feet from where it start

speaker: WS
words: ed there's twenty feet of water, but everything that hits it goes

speaker: WS
words: under, you know, and it keeps raising it up and it'll get built up

speaker: WS
words: til it looks like a mountain, you know. But you get on there and—

speaker: WS
words: four or five men and get slack in front, you know. Oh, maybe the

speaker: WS
words: width of this room, or something like that. Then if it does take a

speaker: WS
words: notion to go it'll leave the crown on piled higher, or swing and go

speaker: WS
words: off. That's the way that works.

speaker: SS
words: What about them jams that don't want to untagle? I know that hap

speaker: SS
words: pens now and then, you'd get one that would just build up real big

speaker: SS
words: and—

speaker: WS
words: Well, you stay with her, if you couldn't use powder. If you could use

speaker: WS
words: powder, use powder. (Loud knock at the door- Visitor: Bill, I don't

speaker: WS
words: know if you've read these or not. If you haven't, why, look 'em over

speaker: WS
words: and if you don't want 'em, throw 'em in the garbage.)

speaker: SS
words: Well known fellow around here.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, he used to run camp for the Potlatch.

speaker: SS
words: I didn't know he run Camp 2.

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yeah. He run some of them donkey camps. He was . Pot

speaker: WS
words: latch Lumber Company, you know, before they consolidated with this

speaker: WS
words: Clearwater Timber Company. That was the Potlatch Lumber Company,

speaker: WS
words: you know.

speaker: SS
words: He was a foreman besides being a—he had a poolhall there?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yeah, he a joint there. His wife run a restaurant and he run

speaker: WS
words: the bar, and the poolhall. Just beer, but he drank whiskey all time,

speaker: WS
words: he always had a bottle of whiskey there, two or three, I don't know

speaker: WS
words: how many. But I wouldn't want to ride with him that day, I was coming

speaker: WS
words: over here I had been working in some of them camps there, Camp

speaker: WS
words: 7 or 5 or maybe, some of 'em. And I quit and. in there in there

speaker: WS
words: in Bovill, and I was coming over here and I was gonna ride with him,

speaker: WS
words: but I didn't get up that morning, and by God, he got killed that same

speaker: WS
words: day.

speaker: SS
words: Driving?

speaker: WS
words: Yeah. The door flew open and he rolled out right under a big semi.

speaker: WS
words: Going around a turn, you know and he maybe leaned a little bit; an

speaker: WS
words: old pickup and the door flew open and he landed right under the

speaker: WS
words: wheel. Yeah, he was a good guy, that guy. But there was some guy,

speaker: WS
words: I can't think of his name now, that run that joint, he isn't there

speaker: WS
words: no more, he's around town, but he— there's someone else running the

speaker: WS
words: joint.

speaker: SS
words: I forget the guy's name, but there's still quite a few people go in

speaker: SS
words: there. People go drink at Bailey's, too, you know.

speaker: WS
words: Bailey's, that's the place. Yeah, that's the place.

speaker: SS
words: Well, there's Bailey's and then there's—

speaker: WS
words: Bailey don't run that no more, does he?

speaker: SS
words: No.

speaker: WS
words: No. Well, that's the guy, an old feller, big, old feller. Drank a

speaker: WS
words: lot of whiskey. Yeah, that's the guy I knew. Yeah, Bailey. Has the

speaker: WS
words: Potlatch got shops there yet?

speaker: SS
words: Gee, I don't know if they got— they got a little operation there,

speaker: SS
words: but I don't know how much of a shop there is, or how big it is. I'm

speaker: SS
words: sure it's nothing like what it used to be.

speaker: WS
words: Oh, no. Used to have an office there. Chet Yanjel did he die?

speaker: SS
words: He's still there.

speaker: WS
words: He retired.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah. Do you know Byers Sanderson?

speaker: SS
words: He's still there and his brother, John, is still there. John is

speaker: SS
words: quite old. Ernie Smith just died. Did you know him?

speaker: SS
words: Ernie Smith was in the shop all the time.

speaker: WS

words: Yeah. They were working for Fromos, most of 'em.

speaker: SS
words: For who?

speaker: WS
words: Frank Fromos. He was a big, big gyppo for the Potlatch.

speaker: SS
words: What about the men that were killed? Did you know 'em pretty well?

speaker: WS
words: I knew 'em just as well as I knew myself. Yeah. One of 'em was

speaker: WS
words: paralyzed, he didn't die right away, he died in the hospital in Orofino.

speaker: WS
words: The other two died, in a matter of days, three.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember these guys, who they were, their names?

speaker: SS
words: Country

speaker: WS
words: Old Mike's the only one I can remember. I can't remember the

speaker: WS
words: rest of them.

speaker: SS
words: County Mike?

speaker: WS
words: Old Country Mike.

speaker: SS
words: Old Country? That's just bad. That's bad.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah. But it's the only way you could get 'er agoin', because we

speaker: WS
words: had no money, you know. The organization was broke, you know. But

speaker: WS
words: there was money coming in all the time from— a lot of it anonymous,

speaker: WS
words: you know. A hundred dollars, two hundred dollars and so on, you.

speaker: WS
words: There was quite a bit of it, enough to keep the thing going. We had

speaker: WS
words: to eat, you know. And, of course, a lot of 'em pulled out, you know.

speaker: WS
words: See, the Coast wasn't on strike, after the strike was six weeks old

speaker: WS
words: or so, a lot of these guys pulled out, went to the coast, went to

speaker: WS
words: work. Which was the only thing they could do. But we still kept

speaker: WS
words: a picket line on here, you know. And the railroad company, the emp-

speaker: WS
words: loyees, they recognized our picket lines, they wouldn't cross

speaker: SS
words: Were many loggers crossing the picket line around here?

speaker: WS
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: They weren't crossing it either? They'd respect it.

speaker: WS
words: No there wasn't a Goddam wheel turning. No.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember the demands? I am sure that sheets would have been

speaker: SS
words: one of them. One of the demands. Right? The sheets.

speaker: WS
words: Yes. Oh, the camps were filthy then.

speaker: SS
words: That's funny, I thought they— You know they like to say they cleaned

speaker: SS
words: 'em up after 1917.

speaker: WS
words: They did. But outfit wasn t started then, you know. This Potlatch,

speaker: WS
words: it didn't start until '26,- '25.

speaker: SS
words: So these camps down here never—

speaker: WS
words: Well, they started out good. Not sheets, but good chuck, good camps

speaker: WS
words: and everything. But they just seemed to deteriorate. Everything

speaker: WS
words: went to hell, you know.. Then they would build an old rag camp for

speaker: WS
words: you to sleep in. First thing you know it was full of holes and ev

speaker: WS
words: erything and no floors. Some of the bunkhouses right on the ground.

speaker: WS
words: And double bunks, you know. They'd settle down and the springs

speaker: WS
words: would be on the ground on the lower bunks, unless you shimmed up

speaker: WS
words: under 'em. Oh, it was awful. Yeah, there was a few concessions any

speaker: WS
words: way. Not directly to us, but that was the result.

speaker: SS
words: Could the— Could the IWW's guys fight back? Was there anything that

speaker: SS
words: they could do besides just run a picket line?

speaker: WS
words: That's all. The law was not with you.

speaker: SS
words: Sure.

speaker: WS
words: They were strictly against you. It ain't like now— These guys on

speaker: WS
words: strike now, they got certain law protection in lots of cases. And

speaker: WS
words: they go beyond that, too; get in trouble.

speaker: SS
words: I guess maybe partly because they don't think of- like the Wobblies

speaker: SS
words: they thought they were threatening worse than they were. These

speaker: SS
words: unions these days are pussy cats, you know. They're not about to

speaker: SS
words: really break things up.

speaker: WS
words: Oh, no. Once in a while— like when they were reorganizing the CIO,

speaker: WS
words: they got rough. ..Ford Motor Company, they had all

speaker: WS
words: kinds of gunmen hired for them. Special police.

speaker: SS
words: Yes. Well they kicked a bunch of - they kicked some of them unions

speaker: SS
words: out when they— it was going on. And some of the better ones, too.

speaker: SS
words: The Longshoremen. The West Coast Longshoremen;/kicked out. You

speaker: SS
words: know the philosophy — the revolutionary philosophy in overthrowing

speaker: SS
words: the government— what was it that the Wobblies figured would be the

speaker: SS
words: new— I mean, what would take its place?

speaker: WS
words: Well, I can't explain that to you. I used to have an idea what they

speaker: WS
words: meant.

speaker: SS
words: I was sort of figuring it would be some kind of workers control of—

speaker: WS
words: Well, yes. Like the different industries, you know, they were an in

speaker: WS
words: dustrial organization, the IWW. They have a head for the automobile

speaker: WS
words: industry, for the lumber industry and a head for transportation,

speaker: WS
words: and they'd all centralize in that— then they'd create a government

speaker: WS
words: that would function over all. I think that's the way it worked out.

speaker: WS
words: I can't think of any other way.

speaker: SS
words: So that the union would run the industry.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, that's right.

speaker: SS
words: You know, there s an old-timer.Potlatch in a sawmill town you know,

speaker: SS
words: milltown, that I talked to — it's funny, 'cause he was telling me

speaker: SS
words: how in the 1917 strike how the company— all the propaganda that

speaker: SS
words: they laid down, all the propaganda about how bad the Bolsheviks and

speaker: SS
words: bomb throwers.were. And he said to me, "We believed that stuff."

speaker: SS
words: And he said, "That at some point, maybe a couple of years," he said,

speaker: SS
words: "I figured out just what they were doing." You know what the com

speaker: SS
words: pany would do and what the newspapers were doing, what the lawmen

speaker: SS
words: were doing. Just how we'd been had. They had given us a whole line

speaker: SS
words: of bullshit all along. And he said, "It just taught me that you can't

speaker: SS
words: believe what you read in the newspapers."

speaker: SS
words: I guess those guys in the sawmills and in the milltowns were—

speaker: SS
words: the company kind of controlled them pretty well.

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yeah. The sawmills they were always apart from the woods until

speaker: WS
words: now. Now they're in with the woods. They all belong to the IWA.

speaker: WS
words: But that's what I argued, in this strike in '47, you know. I argued—

speaker: WS
words: You see, the mill is what put us out on the strike in '47. They went

speaker: WS
words: on strike; the mill. And that's what put us out. I argued to keep

speaker: WS
words: the mill out of our business, you know. 'Course, they couldn't be

speaker: WS
words: cause they all belonged to the same organization. Only different

speaker: WS
words: locals, you know. Our local's at Pierce and their's is down here

speaker: WS
words: at Clarkston. I think that's where the mill local is. So they fin

speaker: WS
words: ally went out first, you see, ahead of ours. Three or four days.

speaker: WS
words: So we had to go. You see the mill that was here long before

speaker: WS
words: we did. And the was right in behind our picket lines

speaker: WS
words: trying to organize those that were working at the time. Can you

speaker: WS
words: imagine that? Oh, it was a mixed up, Goddam, bolixed up affair.

speaker: WS
words: Potlatch Rose, they called her around here stumping around the street

speaker: WS
words: and talking in parks and everyplace. You know she was a woman.

speaker: WS
words: She was company hired, of course. She was a good talker. Oh she

speaker: WS
words: had a line of shit. She'd get an audience spellbound listening to

speaker: WS
words: her, you know.

speaker: SS
words: Was she good looking?

speaker: WS
words: Sure. Hell, yes! Well dressed. (Laughter) I had to laugh, i thought

speaker: WS
words: to myself, ''You Goddamned old son of a bitch, you're alright, tool"

speaker: WS
words: I heard her talk. I was in the mill. A bunch of us came down there

speaker: WS
words: one time. This picket line at the mill wasn't functioning very

speaker: WS
words: good, so they wanted some of us to come down, so we come down here.

speaker: WS
words: And she was out there and she talked right there. Why, Jesus Christ

speaker: WS
words: she could make you believe the Goddam river was running the other

speaker: WS
words: way! (Chuckles) Yeah, I liked her, I thought she was alright, you

speaker: WS
words: know. didn't believe it; she couldn't. She sure boosted that old

speaker: WS
words: Potlatch! (Laughter)

speaker: SS
words: It sounds like they were pretty crafty in knowing how to try —

speaker: WS
words: Ohhhhh! Jesus Christ, some of them personnel managers, you know,

speaker: WS
words: they're pretty smooth operators. And^they got their publicity men,

speaker: WS
words: This Beardsmore, that attorney,

speaker: WS
words: you know. Did you know him?

speaker: SS
words: No. I've heard about him.

speaker: WS
words: He was great. Yeah. I talked to him. We struck on the river here

speaker: WS
words: at different times, you know. Five times, I think we struck on this

speaker: WS
words: river. Won every time. Every time, we won. Strike for a dollar,

speaker: WS
words: you know. Something like that, a dollar a day. Get 'er every time!

speaker: WS
words: Might have to hang up a couple of days, or something like that. But

speaker: WS
words: Beardsmore always come up.

speaker: SS
words: Would this be during the drive?

speaker: WS
words: Yeah. Mutiny Bar, we struck there three times.

speaker: SS
words: Mutiny Bar? (Chuckles)

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, that's right up here. Oh, it's a rock bound son of a bitch of

speaker: WS
words: a place. It's above Junction a ways. Jesus! it's an aw

speaker: WS
words: ful place. Everybody'd be tired and half pissed off, and somebody'd

speaker: WS
words: quit or something and^someone'd say, "Let's go for four bits or a

speaker: WS
words: dollar? It's a good chance." So we'd stack arms, get 'er, you see,

speaker: WS
words: right away; next day. Some of them bigwigs'd come up from the mill,

speaker: WS
words: "Go ahead." That's all there is to it. But it's easy to win on a

speaker: WS
words: drive. I've never seen a driving crew lose yet. No. I've been on

speaker: WS
words: a lot of strikes on drives, you know. But I never did see one lose.

speaker: WS
words: Would these be— you wouldn't go through the union for that, would

speaker: WS
words: you? I mean, that'd just be a bunch of guys.

speaker: WS
words: Well, this is a wildcatter here, yeah.

speaker: WS
words: What was the union? Was that CIO then?

speaker: WS
words: Yeah. The last time we struck.

speaker: WS
words: I guess that's kind of a deal, if you strike when you've got the

speaker: WS
words: cards in there, you can win.

speaker: WS
words: That was the only way. That was the Wobbly theory, "Strike when the

speaker: WS
words: they argued, iron's hot." But, by Jesus— well, this last time, of course,"We

speaker: WS
words: got a contract. We got a contract." I told 'em "To hell with the

speaker: WS
words: contract!" I said,"I've drove on this Goddam river for three dollars

speaker: WS
words: a day, and ten dollars a day and fifteen dollars a day," and I said,

speaker: WS
words: "my labor's worth twenty dollars a day now, before I go another God

speaker: WS
words: damn inch!" Yeah, you could always win on a drive, but them camps,

speaker: WS
words: oh, Jesus, they'd always have a big supply of logs out here every

speaker: WS
words: time we struck, you know. By the time they were used up we were

speaker: WS
words: starved out. Ready to go. See how that works.
